div.note: SECRET
19 Aug 44
SUBJECT :- Morale
To :- Land Forces Adriatic
From :- HQ VIS Bde

Ref your two letters LFA/43/A of 15 July and LFA/44/A of 26 July, I submit morale report for the three months ending 20 Aug 44 for all units at 1 Fwd Base excl 2 SS Bde. hhh 1. Offences against the Army Act
(a) Absence
Under one hour- 12 cases
24 hours - 5 cases
Over 24 - 1 case.
(b) Other offences -
Very few cases, incl 3 in connection with WD vehs, 3 of drunken­ness, 1 of insubordination and 1 of creating a disturbance.
(c) Minor offences
About 25 cases in all. hhh 2. Confidence

(a) In the Army's cause, its leaders, and in the progress of the war. At the present time confidence in the cause and the progress of the war runs high. In one unit disappointment is expressed that after 5 years they are still in uniform, but it is realized that the progress made since 1940 might have taken a good deal longer. This same unit finds it difficult to take a personal interest in the Japanese war, and feels that it is not "our affair". This is indeed a strange view.

Another unit feels that the defeat of the Japanese will take a long time, and that real progress against them cannot be made until the end of the war in Europe. There is general confidence in our leaders, though one unit feels they could do more to curtail length of overseas service.
(b) In amn & eqpt This is complete, and in fact amounts to a certain degree of pride. hhh 3. Contentment
(a) Interest in trg & ops This is considerable, though now that the end of the war appears close, it has been difficult to maintain interest in the more uninteresting and routine forms of trg.
Trg is handicapped in that hardly any mob trg can be carried out on the island; this applies particularly to the Fd Regt RA.
Trg for a particular op is far more popular than other trg.
All ranks are very interested in the ops that are mounted from this base, and are proud to have been selected for service under a Special Service Bde.
So far as AA units are concerned they have engaged more "live" targets since coming to this base than for some time previously.
The AA gunners have shown keenness to be at an instant state of readiness for long periods in order that no opportunity of opening fire will be missed.
(a) Living conditions
(i) Quarters These are a bit rough, but quite sufficient for summer weather and there is no discontent with them.
(ii) Messing There is a universal dislike of dehydrated food, and more fresh fruit, meat, and vegetables are required.
Two units find they do not get enough rations, and one unit considers the rations compare unfavourably with those on the mainland, and those issued to American tps.
One unit asks for more tea, sugar and milk.

One unit considers they have a very high standard of messing, and it is interesting to note that this unit is the only one which mentions that visits from the catering adviser have always been encouraged.
(iii) NAAFI
(a) General This has improved considerably lately, and the more liberal supply of cordials is very popular.
There are two main complaints. One is the shortage of beer which never exceeds one bottle, and is often half a bottle, or even nil, per week.
This complaint has weight added to it by the universal knowledge that RN personnel stationed at this base get a bottle a day.
The other complaint is the high price of some commodities. For instance 3d for a cup of tea from the mob van, is too much.
One unit suggests that more of the NAAFI profits should go to reduction of overseas prices.
(b) Officers' Shop Officers complain that officers' shops are very poorly stocked. Shoes, stockings, badges of rank, handkerchiefs, etc are rarely obtainable.

(iv) Recreation & amusements General satisfaction is expressed with the organized games and sports. Owing to the infrequency of moves, all units at the base have got to know each other and have developed a healthy rivalry.

One unit reports a shortage of footballs and wireless sets, and regrets that the issue of PT shoes has ceased. It does seem that there ought to be some form of official footwear other than GS boots for PT, sports, etc.
The regular cinema shows are appreciated, though better films are asked for. Educational films are not popular.
The two ENSA shows were well attended and enjoyed, but no unit speaks highly of them, and the general opinion seems to be that their subject matter is too "trivial".
(v) Leave More vacancies are required as the present rate of turnover is too slow. This is aggravated by the uncertainties of tpt to the mainland.
One unit does not like rest camps and would like to get away from the Army when on leave.

Two units consider they are entitled to home leave after the end of the war in Europe, and before being sent to the Far East. (vi) Mail Though there used to be considerable delays, most units are now well satisfied with the speed of delivery.

Two units ask for a larger distribution of Air Letters. One of them cannot understand why the aircraft which bring an unrestricted quantity of air letters from home, cannot take the same unrestricted quantity back to England.
The shortage in the issue is particularly felt when there is trouble at home, and the soldier wishes to write to several people quickly.

(c) Finance - pay, allowances & expenses The men are mostly satisfied since the present pay increases. There are one or two complaints however, and instances are quoted where the increases have had the effect of making the pay of a junior offr or soldier about the same as that of a higher rank or tradesman, who, by virtue of his additional responsibilities, should get more.

Instances are - A married offr with one child gets only 6d a day more as a capt than as a lieutenant, after income tax has been paid. A sgt and lance sgt get only 2d a day more than their next juniors, after payment of tax. A gunner class 1A gets the dame pay (5/- per day) as a Dvr Op Class III. The dvr op has to be an efficient signaller as well as dvr and should get more. Two units state that dissatisfaction has been caused by the delay in receiving notification of balances and changes in rates of pay, and also by inaccuracies in such notifications from Paymasters. One unit reports bitter feeling over the higher rates of pay of Allied Armies. One unit raises the old but pressing question of the underpayment of dvrs, when the value of the eqpt they are responsible for is taken into consideration. It is pointed out that if a dvr causes damage to his veh he is quickly enough mulcted in stoppages, but that there is no compensating payment or bonus if he preserves his veh from damage. A "non accident bonus" is suggested after a certified period of accident free driving.
(d) Conditions affecting families
General anxiety is reported over the flying bomb raids, partic­ularly as to the financial position when a mortgaged house is destroyed.
Some men are worried at the high prices for necessities such as childrens' clothing now prevailing at home, and there is a fear that rents may be high after the war.

One unit reports several cases of marriages breaking up through long years of separation, and that the husbands felt that their marriages could have been saved had they been able to get home on a compassionate posting.

Another unit suggests that men who have applied for a compassionate posting home should be kept infm of the progress of the application. It is suggested that it would save them from worry, and that it would be a help to them, if the numbers sent home each month on compassionate postings could be published.
One unit reports that feeling against the Americans at home still runs high.
(e) Relations between offrs, NCOs & men Without exception these are good.
(f) Relations with other services, etc
(i) RAF Those units who have had contact with the RAF report that their relations with them are good.
One unit, which sees a lot of the RAF, states that relations are "Better than they have ever been".
(ii) ATS There are none here.
(iii) Dominion & USA tps
Those units who have met any report good relations, though one unit excepts the Americans.
(iv) Yugoslav civilians & National Army Relations are really excellent. hhh 4. Education and ABCA
Recently there has been an increased interest in post-war problems.
Otherwise not much interest is shown in education, apart from individuals who take voluntary language classes, etc.
It is not always possible to run ABCA discussions, but where this is done, they are appreciated; more so if they are held during normal working hours.
One unit suggests that "Current Affairs" is not successful, and that "World Press Review" is much better.
One unit states that education has been more popular than ABCA. hhh 5. Efficiency
(a) Physical fitness This is very good; there is plenty of sport on the island, and the swimming is very popular. The sickness rate is very low.
(b) Discipline This is good, apart from a tendency lately to slackness in dress which is probably due to the hot weather. This is rapidly being put right. hhh 6. Miscellaneous
Two units report considerable apathy in filling in the electoral registration forms.

Two units report that their men were, and still are, proud and interested in their unusual role in the defence of this island. At the same time they anticipate that the interest is likely to be difficult to maintain for much longer, though the pride in their past share in the island def will remain.
I feel that as long as the fighting units on the Island can always be planning and looking forward to some future operation their morale will remain at its present high pitch.
Without exception every man that I talk to, going round the Island, always seems happy, fit, and in exceptionally good heart.
Colonel
Comd VIS Bde
CMF
